# The Effect of Performance Feedback on Employee Motivation: A Field Experiment

## Abstract

We conduct a randomized field experiment with 2,400 employees across 12 firms to test the causal effect of performance feedback frequency on employee motivation and productivity. Results show that weekly feedback increases productivity by 8.3% compared to quarterly feedback.

## 1. Introduction

Performance feedback is a widely used management practice, but causal evidence on its effectiveness is limited. We use a randomized controlled trial to identify the causal effect of feedback frequency.

## 2. Experimental Design

### 2.1 Sample and Randomization

We recruited 2,400 employees from 12 firms in the technology and finance sectors. Employees within each firm were randomly assigned to one of three conditions:
- Weekly feedback (N = 800)
- Monthly feedback (N = 800)
- Quarterly feedback (control, N = 800)

Randomization was stratified by firm, department, and baseline performance. Balance checks confirm that observables are well-balanced across conditions (F-test p = 0.42).

### 2.2 Intervention

Employees in the treatment groups received structured performance feedback from their direct supervisors at the assigned frequency. Feedback sessions followed a standardized protocol covering: goal progress, behavioral observations, and development suggestions.

### 2.3 Measurement

Our primary outcome is productivity measured as output per hour, collected from firm administrative records. We also measure self-reported motivation using the Intrinsic Motivation Inventory (IMI; Ryan & Deci, 2000), administered at baseline, midpoint (3 months), and endline (6 months).

## 3. Results

### 3.1 Main Effects

Weekly feedback significantly increases productivity by 8.3% (p < 0.01, 95% CI [5.7%, 10.9%]) compared to quarterly feedback. Monthly feedback increases productivity by 3.1% (p = 0.04, 95% CI [0.2%, 6.0%]). The difference between weekly and monthly feedback is also significant (p = 0.02).

### 3.2 Robustness

Results are robust to firm fixed effects, department-level clustering of standard errors, and exclusion of outliers. A randomization inference test with 10,000 permutations confirms the significance of the main treatment effect (p = 0.008).

## 4. Discussion and Conclusion

Weekly feedback causally improves productivity. The effect size is economically meaningful: an 8.3% increase represents approximately $4,200 additional output per employee per year. Limitations include the six-month study duration and the focus on technology/finance sectors.
